fund.name: Motilal Oswal Midcap Fund-Direct Plan-Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap stocks offer higher growth than large caps over long periods. This fund has consistent 5-year returns and is from a trusted fund house. At 40% allocation, it drives growth while staying within moderate risk.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Flexi cap funds invest across market sizes, automatically balancing risk. HDFC's fund is large, stable, and has delivered steady returns. 25% allocation ensures diversification. We use your ₹2L lumpsum here to seed the portfolio.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Combines stability of large companies with growth of mid-caps. This fund has outperformed peers over 3 and 5 years. 20% allocation adds balance and complements the mid-cap fund.

fund.name: WhiteOak Capital ELSS Tax Saver Fund Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: ELSS funds give tax savings under Section 80C. You're in the 20% tax slab, so you save ₹10,500/year in tax (on ₹52.5k/year investment). This fund has strong 3-year returns and low risk. 15% allocation maxes your 80C benefit efficiently.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin by Zerodha.
Step 2: Complete your KYC if not already done (takes 5 mins with PAN & Aadhaar).
Step 3: Start monthly SIPs: ₹14,000 in Motilal Oswal Midcap, ₹7,000 in Motilal Oswal Large & Midcap, ₹5,250 in WhiteOak ELSS.
Step 4: Invest ₹2 L lumpsum in HDFC Flexi Cap fund.
Step 5: Set calendar reminders for SIP dates and annual review.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: check all SIPs are running and funds are reflecting in your account.
After 1 year: compare each fund’s performance vs category average. If any fund is in bottom 25% for 3 years, consider switching.
Every 3 years: review if your risk appetite or goals have changed.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your portfolio mix. If any category (e.g., mid cap) has grown so much that it’s more than 5% above your target (e.g., above 45% instead of 40%), sell some and move to underweight categories. This keeps risk in check.
plan.rationale: At 31, with a 22-year horizon, you can afford to have 85% in stocks for growth. Your ₹35k/month surplus and ₹8L corpus are enough to target ₹4Cr in 22 years. We used ELSS for 15% to save tax (₹10.5k/year for you), and avoided complex or thematic funds as you prefer simplicity. Mid and large & mid-cap funds drive growth, while flexi cap adds stability. Your joint home loan means we kept risk 'moderate' — not too aggressive.
risks: Stock markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will go down temporarily. This is normal and recovers over time.
Mid-cap funds are riskier than large caps — they can fall more in downturns but grow faster over time.
ELSS has a 3-year lock-in: you can't withdraw that money early.
Tax rules or fund managers can change — we’ll adjust if needed.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: 100 % of the equity sleeve is in actively-managed funds (Mid-cap, Flexi-cap, Large-&-Mid-cap, ELSS); no index funds or ETFs appear → 0.0. Rebalancing is suggested only once a year with a ±5 %-band and 3-year goal review → high turnover_score (0.85). Expense ratios (1–1.15 %) are acknowledged but never used to screen or prefer lower-cost options; the rationale cites past returns and manager pedigree → cost_emphasis_score 0.05. Every rationale is built on fund-specific research (past alpha, peer ranking, fund-house reputation) with no mention of broad-market indices → research_vs_cost_score 0.0. The plan is explicitly framed for a 22-year horizon, kids' college and retirement, with gradual derisking decades away → time_horizon_alignment 0.95. Portfolio-weighted metrics show beta≈1.0 but very high tracking error (11 %-plus) and strong positive alpha, indicating genuine active stock-picking rather than closet-index behaviour → portfolio_activeness_score 0.05.

pqs.reasoning:
goal_alignment: 85 % equity for a 22-year horizon sounds growth-oriented, but the portfolio ignores the 5-year home-down-payment goal that needs capital safety; no debt or arbitrage sleeve is provided for that short horizon. The glide-path begins only at year 10, leaving the 60-L need in 5 years unaddressed.
diversification: All four funds are equity-oriented, three from the same fund house (Motilal Oswal) and two in the mid-cap space (Mid-cap & Large-&-Mid); zero exposure to large-cap index, debt, arbitrage, gold, or other fund houses, creating concentration risk.
risk_return_appropriateness: Projected 16.8 % CAGR is optimistic for a moderate-risk 20 % slab investor, especially with 65 % in mid-cap segments; no stress test or linkage to the imminent home-loan payout; 0 % debt makes the plan aggressive rather than moderate.
internal_consistency: Claims "moderate" risk yet allocates 65 % to mid-cap funds and 0 % to debt; lumpsum is routed to Flexi-cap but rationale cites "stability", while the mid-cap sleeve is larger; glide-path shown but not implemented in current allocation.
tax_efficiency: Correctly uses ELSS (15 %) to exhaust 80C for a 20 % slab investor; all holdings are equity-oriented so gains will enjoy favourable 12.5 % LTCG instead of slab taxation, meeting the investor’s preference for tax-efficient instruments.
